course: Codes and Standards Definitions and History
primary_case: Helicopter swashplate bearing failure (Kaydon Reali-Slim bearing, unnamed heavy-lift helicopter manufacturer)
secondary_cases: V-22 Osprey Ship 4 crash court of inquiry
Japanese Maritime Defense Forces helicopter loss in Sea of Japan
Grenada invasion aircraft carrier steam-system repair (1983)
framing_topics: Precision manufacturing tolerances (1 part in 10,000; 50 millionths of an inch)
Closed vs open conformity in ball bearings
Brinelling and witness marks as forensic evidence
Board of inquiry procedure and rigging
Emergency Powers Act and FBI takeover of supplier facility
Engineering as dealing with complexity and ambiguity
History of the word 'engineer' and MIT's role in defining engineering disciplines
organizations_named: Marine Corps Air Station Cherry Point
editorial_flags: p3 'swashblade' → 'swashplate' bracketed; Tom uses 'swatch plate' habitually thereafter (preserved as Tom's pronunciation, not a slip)
p32 captioner garbled 'thousandths' for 'millionths' at multiple points; corrected with brackets
p61 'we scrapped it three weeks' missing 'ago'; bracketed
p67 'fire in the cell' captioner error for 'nacelle'; corrected silently
p71 'Professor Sataway' — name uncertain; flagged for editor (possible Saadeh or similar MIT colleague Tom refers to in the etymology aside)
notes_for_layer3: Lecture is structurally one long case study (helicopter swashplate failure) with two codas (V-22 Osprey court of inquiry; history of 'engineer'). Proposed section count: 7. Tom's habitual 'swatch plate' for 'swashplate' should be regularized to 'swashplate' in layer 3 (it is consistent pronunciation drift, not a one-time slip; reader-facing text should use the technical term).
